messages.author: Uncle Lit
messages.text: Hi Andrew! Welcome to meshcore! SF11 and BW250 might have a longer range in an isolated 2-node test. But that is just a setting that we borrowed from meshtastic when we didn't know better. the narrow bandwidth in the USA/Canada (Recommended) preset definitely works better with a couple repeaters in place. SF7 is a lot more responsive in message delivery, it feels almost like using iMessage, SMS, Signals, etc. With narrow bandwidth at 62.5, it fits in between all the water and power meters that are in use everywhere in north America (USA and Canada). The interference signals from these water and power meters are especially detrimental to high elevation repeaters because these repeaters will hear everything including all these interference signals within tens of miles, so narrow bandwidth ends up working better than alternate (really should be named deprecated). The biggest meshcore meshes around the world, Puget Sound + Salish Mesh (Vancouver, Victoria, Seattle, Tacoma, Portland), Boston, Austin, UK, EU, Australia, New Zealand, Czech, are all on narrow 62.5 bandwidth. Take a look at the puget sound mesh map here: https://analyzer.letsme.sh/map?lat=47.6&long=-122.3&zoom=8 another reason to use Recommended is because MeshCore can have up to 64 hops (vs meshtastic's 7-hop limit), there is a very good chance you can link up to your neighboring cities' mesh if you are on the same preset. By using the same Recommend preset, we can send and receive messages from Vancouver, BC, to Victoria, Seattle, Tacoma, Olympia, down to Portland and Eugene, OR. In a few more months, may be a year, we are confident we will link up to the Bay Area in California.

messages.text: Thanks so much for the info! That makes a lot of sense especially about the narrow bandwidth. messages.author: Uncle Lit
messages.text: since 62.5 is much narrower than 250, we compensate it with SF7 to get the speed back and then some. that is why narrow is so fast. with higher SF number, it takes longer to transmit. another advantage of faster transmission is that you can get off the airwave much faster, giving other nodes opportunities to send packets out and further minimizing packet collisions. It makes for a faster, healthier mesh. LoRa is actually very resilient. we have had 150 miles transmissions from Victoria, BC to our main repeater on Cougar Mountain in Seattle. The key is having clear line of sight and high elavation.

messages.author: Andrew L - Central Texas Mesh
messages.text: Yeah. Same boat here. There are still only just a few of us. There does reach a point at which it is so slow that it is difficult to use, especially if you are going to use remote administration, or multiple hops. At this point, we are using BW 62.5, SF9, variable coding rate depending on use case. It has decently a long range, but isn’t so slow that it’s unbearable. We will probably just keep adjusting the settings as the mesh grows, and we get more repeaters on genuine towers. I’m going to install one on a 100’ tower here soon, which should really help. I still need to get some sort of spectrum analyzer to figure out if we should use a frequency other than the US default. Let us know how it goes for you!

Yeah. Same boat here. There are still only just a few of us. There does reach a point at which it is so slow that it is difficult to use, especially if you are going to use remote administration, or multiple hops. At this point, we are using BW 62.5, SF9, variable coding rate depending on use case. It has decently a long range, but isn’t so slow that it’s unbearable. messages.author: garrisonsan
messages.text: With region filtering/scopes becoming part of the main client app ( https://buymeacoffee.com/ripplebiz/region-filtering) and hopefully with more mesh growth in the state, I'd like to suggest the following naming conventions of scopes for repeater owners to add to their repeaters as desired so that at least we have a standardized way of doing things: #texas - to pass on any traffic tagged for a statewide channel/mesh, if one ever develops #abcreg - a regional area scope using the name of the most recognized or generally popular city or colloquial term (e.g. #dfwreg for Dallas/Ft. Worth, #houreg for Houston, maybe #capreg - capitol region - for Austin/San Antonio, etc.). Smaller cities can still take advantage of this, too. I'd suggest using the last three characters of the ICAO code for the nearest regional airport (e.g. #lbbreg for Lubbock, #mafreg for Midland/Odessa, #amareg for amarillo, #spsreg for Wichita Falls, etc.) #hometownco - county level scope where needed #hometownco-east - geographical subdivision of a county level scope #cityname - city level scope #cityname-north - geographical subdivision of a city level scope As the linked article outlines, so long as repeater owners don't remove the "*" scope (present by default) from their repeaters, all flood packets received will still be forwarded, so there's no downside to planning ahead and setting up scopes early at the local/regional level. Then if/when traffic management becomes an issue, removing the wildcard scope is an easy change to make.

messages.author: garrisonsan
messages.text: You might have it backwards? Assuming I've understood it correctly after watching Andy's demo video a while back & reading the available docs/posts on the topic, If the wildcard (asterisk) entry that's present by default is removed, then the region list turns into an allow list, meaning that flood packets tagged with stuff not matching an item in the list will not be repeated. The answer, regardless, is coordination. Let's say the mesh grew to cover most of DFW and hopped along I35 all the way down to San Antonio (including most of Austin) and that network congestion was becoming a problem. Additionally, let's say the DFW group collectively moved the bulk of their chats and beacons to #dfwreg and Austin/San Antonio to #capreg. Both groups left #texas as a routable entry and removed the wildcard. If the folks in Waco or Killeen wanted to participate in both regional chats instead of picking one or falling back to #texas, they would need to coordinate with repeater owners along the chain south from DFW and with repeater owners along the chain north from Austin to make sure the packets from those respective regions were still being repeated. The folks in Waco/Killeen would then add both regions and #texas as routable entries and have an overlap zone locally for #dfwreg and #capreg. Packets for #texas would repeat and pass through. I'm not sure how practical the hypothetical is, since it intuitively would be likely to leave Killeen/Waco with an unusable mesh unless they repeater link to a higher bandwidth local mesh on a separate frequency, but nonetheless structurally that's my understanding of how it could be implemented.

messages.author: bp-gone
messages.text: I think you stated the problem... folks in some areas would be beholden to the coordination and good faith efforts of many people (repeater owners). The whole reason to have these repeaters killing packets is to stop the flow, and no matter where you draw the line, there could be someone on the other side of that line who wants that packet. Which brings me back to my opinion on this. It's (a) a new feature that (b) could change and (c) we don't require it yet for large mesh operations, so I'd say "wait and see". Do not spend a lot of effort and messing around with repeater settings when there's no benefit yet. At least there's no use case for greater Austin yet (I find it humorous that some consider San Antonio part of the Capitol region? They are their own region and have very powerful meshes as far as I know). Things may be different in, say, DFW, so I'd want to see them implement it there to divide Dallas and Fort Worth, for example, and report back with hard data and lessons learned. The Austin Mesh is all about inclusion right now, we don't even have recommended hashtag channels.

messages.author: Eric
messages.text: 10dB is great but my main worry is looking down the road at what we're actually trying to build and some inevitabilities. The out of box experience 910 is literally labeled US Recommended in the UI, that alone guarantees a significant number of users are just going to click that default and never think of it again. If new users see zero replies because the region 'coordinated' 907, they'll assume the network is dead, and just chuck the radio in a drawer forever because they aren't looking for our discord or website or even think there's any reason to try the other frequencies playing trial and error. Haven't we actually already seen this happen, fragmenting the network and stalling the community? ​This thread has some great points about inclusion and coordination, if relying on repeater owners to manually coordinate software hashtag filters is already a nightmare , then hiding our whole network on a custom 907 frequency is infinitely worse . Advanced users might do deep CLI tinkering, but most people are going to run default. ​Besides, is any region crowded enough to warrant an allow list? Cascadia Mesh spans over 300 miles from the northern California border up through Vancouver Canada and is reported to max around 14 hops. Also why would we NOT want statewide chatter? Isn't the whole point of meshcore to use those 64 hops and build a massive connected network like Cascadia mesh? If that's the goal then ANY frequency will get crowded eventually. ​I see how 907 could feel awesome now, but the only guarantee it seems to provide is fragmenting any local user base, not to mention that we'll probably lose that 10dB quiet zone to migrating meshtastic traffic in the near future anyway if that FCC petition for 5G goes through. I just can't help feel it makes more sense to just align with the 910 default and trust meshcore's routing to filter the noise so we don't bleed out all the new users? (edited)

messages.author: NoahK
messages.text: Lora uses frequency chirps for each bit, and at different spreading factors with the same bandwidth, these chirps happen at different (multiples of 2) rates. A receiver configured to listen in on one spreading factor is effectively tuned out of the other spreading factor (cross-correlation <10%), even if they share the same frequency center. https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/stamp/stamp.jsp?tp=&arnumber=10609524 , Figure 12. There is also research concluding that the orthogonality is not perfect, but if the lora preamble is successfully rx'd, then the rest of the packet will be too.

messages.author: Eric
messages.text: This is the closest we have to a regional discord, it's a great place to get started but I would also join Austin mesh discord since we're trying to establish a reliable link between Austin and San Antonio. If you can make it to blanco BBQ in one hour for the ham radio club meeting, that's an amazing in person start. I won't be at tonight's meeting but I will be at next meeting and I'm set to speak at the BSides conference in June to give a meshcore presentation which I'd love for you to join us there. I'm also hosting an open to the public "Mesh 101 & build workshop" at UTSA main campus April 25th at 1PM. All of meshcore is built by the community, so it's up to people like us to take the initiative to get involved in the community to make it grow. That's why I joined the ham radio club because I don't know anything about calculatin link budgets or best practices for maximizing coverage given the hardware we're working with. Having someone with your experience is absolutely invaluable to building meshcore infrastructure, and I'd love for you to help us any way you can! (edited)

messages.author: Eric
messages.text: Howdy y'all, the San Antonio regional mesh is officially kicking into high gear! I’ve been doing my best spreading excitement and interest to build out a city-wide infrastructure for Bexar County, inspired by what the Austin Mesh, Cascadia Mesh, and other similar regional mesh crews have pulled off. To get this moving, our newly chartered UTSA Mesh Infrastructure Society is hosting our first official hardware build workshop, and we'd love for local builders, hackers, and off-grid enthusiasts to come down and build with us! Saturday, April 25 | 1:30 PM - 3:30 PM (Please kindly aim for 1:00 PM arrival to park, navigate campus to the room, and talk shop before we start!) UTSA H-E-B Student Union, Hidalgo Room Admission is open to the public and free! 1:30 PM: A quick presentation on why we are deploying MeshCore for Bexar County's infrastructure instead of Meshtastic. 2:00 PM: Hands-on flashing and assembly lab. Got gear gathering dust? If you have a LilyGO, Heltec, or RAK board sitting in a drawer because you haven't had the time to figure out the hardware assembly, bring it! We’ll walk you through building it out, get it cased up, quickly flash it to MeshCore, and have it running on the network before you leave. Even if you're not building a radio, we'd still love to have you! Come out to learn about LoRa and MeshCore, talk shop, or collaborate on strategy for spreading local interest and finding optimal node placements. We are providing the room and build guidance, but participants need to bring their own hardware: Laptop & Cable: Windows or Mac (no iPads) + a verified data-capable USB cable. Radio Hardware: Unassembled board, case, & antenna. Tools: Precision Phillips (#00, #0, or #1) and small metric Allen keys (1.5mm, 2.0mm, and 2.5mm) for your case hardware. https://rowdylink.utsa.edu/event/12415109 Looking forward to connecting, (Cue the Jeff Bridges voice) both on and off the grid!
